h1: The DOE advanced reactor pilot program: the 11 projects and the July 4, 2026 criticality target
meta_title: DOE Advanced Reactor Pilot Program: The 11 Projects
meta_description: The DOE Reactor Pilot Program authorizes 11 advanced reactors under EO 14301 to reach criticality by July 4, 2026. Projects, authority, NRC interface, status.
tldr: The DOE Advanced Reactor Pilot Program is the Department of Energy expedited authorization pathway, established by Executive Order 14301 on May 23, 2025, for at least three privately funded test reactors to reach criticality outside the national labs by July 4, 2026. DOE selected 11 projects from 10 companies on August 12, 2025. Authority rests on the Atomic Energy Act Sections 31, 91, and 110 under 10 CFR Part 830, not on NRC licensing. Antares Nuclear Mark-0 was the first to reach criticality on June 3, 2026.
canonical_summary:
The DOE Advanced Reactor Pilot Program is the Department of Energy's expedited authorization pathway for at least three privately funded test reactors to reach criticality by July 4, 2026, outside the national laboratories. It was established by Executive Order 14301, "Reforming Nuclear Reactor Testing at the Department of Energy," signed by President Trump on May 23, 2025 as part of a four order package targeting a quadrupling of installed U.S. nuclear capacity from roughly 100 GW to 400 GW by 2050. DOE issued the Request for Application on June 18, 2025 and announced 11 selected projects from 10 companies on August 12, 2025.

Authorization rests on DOE's standalone Atomic Energy Act authority under Section 31, Section 91, and Section 110, implemented through 10 CFR Part 830 and DOE-STD-3009, the same regime that has governed more than 70 reactors at Idaho National Laboratory and Oak Ridge over eight decades. The Nuclear Regulatory Commission is not in the critical path for first criticality, but its 2019 MOU with DOE, expanded by Addendum No. 9 on October 24, 2025, and its April 2, 2026 proposed rule amending 10 CFR Section 50.43 and 10 CFR Section 53.440 make pilot data a formal evidentiary shortcut for follow on commercial licensing.

By June 2026, Antares Nuclear's Mark-0 had achieved first criticality at Idaho National Laboratory's RACE facility on June 3, 2026, and Valar Atomics' NOVA test assembly had reached criticality at Los Alamos National Laboratory's NCERC on November 17, 2025. The pilot is the headline proof point of the May 23, 2025 executive order package and the principal stress test of whether DOE authorization can seed a commercial advanced reactor fleet without displacing the NRC.

sections.heading: What the pilot is and where it came from
paragraphs: On May 23, 2025, President Donald J. Trump signed four executive orders intended to re establish the United States as the global leader in nuclear energy. The package set a target of quadrupling installed U.S. nuclear capacity from roughly 100 GW to 400 GW by 2050, with interim milestones of 5 GW of power uprates on the existing fleet and 10 new large reactors under construction by 2030.

The Reactor Pilot Program flows directly from one of those orders, Executive Order 14301, "Reforming Nuclear Reactor Testing at the Department of Energy." Section 5(a) of EO 14301 directs the Secretary of Energy to approve at least three reactors under the pilot with the goal of achieving criticality in each of the three by July 4, 2026. The order also compresses the legacy DOE reactor authorization workflow from more than 1,500 pages and 17 sequential steps down to roughly 600 pages and 11 steps.

DOE's Office of Nuclear Energy moved quickly. It issued the Request for Application on June 18, 2025, closed the initial application window on July 21, 2025, and announced initial selections of 11 projects from 10 companies on August 12, 2025. Applicants bear the full lifecycle cost of design, manufacturing, construction, operations, and decommissioning. DOE provides the regulatory pathway, assistance teams, and authorization, not capital.
bullets: Legal basis: Executive Order 14301, Section 5(a), signed May 23, 2025
Companion orders: EO 14302 (industrial base), Deploying Advanced Nuclear Reactor Technologies (federal sites), and the NRC reform order
RFA issued: June 18, 2025
Initial selections announced: August 12, 2025
Criticality target: July 4, 2026, for at least three of the 11 projects
Cost model: applicants fund design through decommissioning; DOE provides authorization and assistance teams

sections.heading: The 11 selected projects
paragraphs: DOE selected 11 projects from 10 companies on August 12, 2025. Oklo received two awards, including one for its subsidiary Atomic Alchemy. All projects share the July 4, 2026 criticality goal by program design. The list below uses the project names and ratings disclosed by DOE and the developers.

subsections.heading: 1. Aalo atomics Aalo-X critical test Reactor
paragraphs: Sub 20 MWth sodium cooled, UO2 fueled thermal spectrum test reactor mirroring the commercial Aalo-1 (10 MWe). Site: Idaho National Laboratory, in the first new reactor building at INL in 50 years. DOE Idaho approved the Documented Safety Analysis on April 30, 2026. Operational readiness review under way. Target criticality: July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 2. Antares Nuclear Mark-0
paragraphs: 500 kWt sodium heat pipe cooled microreactor. Site: INL's Reactor and Critical Experiment (RACE) facility. Achieved first criticality on June 3, 2026, the first private advanced reactor to reach criticality under the pilot and the 53rd reactor built at INL since 1951.

subsections.heading: 3. Atomic Alchemy (oklo subsidiary) VIPR
paragraphs: Versatile Isotope Production Reactor, a 15 MWt light water isotope production reactor. Site characterization began at INL in June 2025. Target criticality: July 4, 2026, with isotope production ramping later.

subsections.heading: 4. Deep Fission borehole reactor-1
paragraphs: 15 MWe pressurized water reactor emplaced approximately one mile underground in a 30 inch borehole. Site: Great Plains Industrial Park, Parsons, Kansas. Groundbreaking December 9, 2025. Target criticality: July 4, 2026, pending DOE authorization.

subsections.heading: 5. Last Energy PWR-5
paragraphs: 5 MWe scaled pilot of the commercial PWR-20 pressurized water microreactor. Site: Texas A&M University System's RELLIS campus, Bryan, Texas. Fuel procured, Other Transaction Agreement signed, licensing submissions filed. Backed by a $100 million Series C. Target criticality: by July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 6. Natura Resources MSR-1
paragraphs: 1 MWth liquid fueled molten salt research reactor. Site: Abilene Christian University's Science and Engineering Research Center, Abilene, Texas. OTA executed December 2025. DOE conditionally committed to supplying HALEU and enriched molten salt. Target criticality: by July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 7. Oklo Aurora-INL
paragraphs: Sodium cooled fast reactor with metal fuel derived from EBR-II, rated up to 75 MWe in commercial form. Site: Idaho National Laboratory. DOE approved the Preliminary Documented Safety Analysis in 2026. Groundbreaking with Kiewit took place in September 2025. Target criticality: July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 8. Oklo second project (aurora fuel fabrication facility lineage)
paragraphs: Reported as a separate Oklo award. The Aurora Fuel Fabrication Facility at INL received DOE Preliminary Documented Safety Analysis approval in December 2025, the first facility approved under the Fuel Line Pilot Program. Target criticality: July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 9. Radiant industries Kaleidos demonstration unit
paragraphs: 1 MWe portable high temperature gas cooled microreactor, helium coolant, TRISO fuel, graphite moderated. Site: INL's DOME facility, the repurposed EBR-II containment, taken over by Radiant on April 1, 2026 for a one year fueled test campaign. The company closed a $300 million plus raise in December 2025. Target criticality: originally July 4, 2026, with most recent guidance pointing to Q4 2026.

subsections.heading: 10. Terrestrial Energy project TETRA
paragraphs: Pilot reactor supporting the 195 MWe Integral Molten Salt Reactor (IMSR) commercial plant, using SALEU fuel. OTA executed January 6, 2026. A commercial IMSR site was separately announced at Texas A&M RELLIS in February 2025, and Westinghouse signed on for the IMSR fuel pilot plant. Target criticality: July 4, 2026.

subsections.heading: 11. Valar atomics ward 250
paragraphs: 100 kWt high temperature gas cooled reactor with TRISO fuel, helium coolant, and graphite moderator. Site: Utah San Rafael Energy Lab (USREL), Emery County, Utah, under the Utah Office of Energy Development. Reactor airlifted to Utah in early 2026. A related test assembly, NOVA Core, achieved criticality at Los Alamos National Laboratory's NCERC on November 17, 2025, the first criticality under the pilot program at a national lab criticality facility. Ward 250 power criticality target: 2026.

sections.heading: DOE authorization versus NRC licensing: what the pilot bypasses
paragraphs: Under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, as amended, U.S. nuclear reactor oversight is bifurcated. Commercial power reactors and most non federal research reactors fall under Nuclear Regulatory Commission licensing pursuant to Sections 103 and 104. Reactors owned or controlled by DOE for research, development, and demonstration are authorized under a separate statutory pathway that bypasses NRC commercial licensing.

AEA Section 31 (42 U.S.C. Section 2051) authorizes DOE to conduct, assist, and foster research and development on atomic energy, including the construction and operation of experimental reactors. AEA Section 91 (42 U.S.C. Section 2121) supports atomic energy programs. AEA Section 110 (42 U.S.C. Section 2140) explicitly exempts DOE from the Section 101 NRC licensing requirement for activities under DOE contracts. The Energy Reorganization Act of 1974 and the DOE Organization Act of 1977 reinforce that the Secretary of Energy is self regulatory for defense and research nuclear facilities.

DOE exercises this authority through 10 CFR Part 830, "Nuclear Safety Management." Subpart B requires contractors to prepare a Documented Safety Analysis (DSA) and derive Technical Safety Requirements (TSRs). Under 10 CFR 830.207, the cognizant DOE Field Office and Headquarters program office review and approve the safety basis and issue a Safety Evaluation Report (SER). DSA preparation typically follows DOE-STD-3009 and, for reactor projects, DOE-STD-1189. DOE Orders 420.1C (Facility Safety) and 414.1D (Quality Assurance) sit on top of the CFR. The historical precedent is deep: more than 70 reactors at Idaho National Laboratory and Oak Ridge have operated under this regime, including EBR-I, EBR-II, the Materials Test Reactor, TREAT, the Advanced Test Reactor, the X-10 Graphite Reactor, the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment, and the High Flux Isotope Reactor.
bullets: Statutory exemption: AEA Section 110 carves DOE activities out of NRC Section 101 licensing
Implementing regulation: 10 CFR Part 830, Subpart B (Safety Basis)
Methodology: DOE-STD-3009 (DSA), DOE-STD-1189 (safety in design)
Approval artifact: DOE Safety Evaluation Report plus approved DSA and TSRs (the authorization basis)
Oversight: DOE Office of Enterprise Assessments and, for defense facilities, the Defense Nuclear Facilities Safety Board under 42 U.S.C. Section 2286
Precedent: 52 reactors built at INL since 1951; HFIR and ATR still operating under 10 CFR Part 830 safety bases

sections.heading: What NRC interaction still matters
paragraphs: DOE authorization removes the NRC from the critical path for first criticality of a pilot reactor, but it does not remove the NRC from the commercial fleet or from several adjacent regulatory domains while the pilot is operating.

Special nuclear material and fuel cycle interfaces, including enrichment, fabrication, and transport of HALEU and used fuel handling, frequently fall under NRC jurisdiction through 10 CFR Parts 70, 71, and 73 even when the reactor is DOE authorized. Export controls under 10 CFR Part 110 and security and safeguards reviews for downstream commercial deployment remain NRC administered. Section 202 of the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974 reserved certain DOE demonstration reactors of sufficient scale to NRC jurisdiction, so the boundary between DOE authorizable and NRC licensable demonstrations is fact specific. The primary purpose test is the operative line: NRC jurisdiction attaches whenever a reactor's primary purpose is commercial or utility scale power generation, so grid sales that are more than incidental tip a pilot out of the DOE pathway.

The operational bridge is the 2019 NRC DOE Memorandum of Understanding on advanced reactors, expanded by Addendum No. 9 signed October 24, 2025 and posted November 3, 2025 (ADAMS ML25303A288). The addendum implements Executive Orders 14299, 14300, 14301, and 14302. DOE NE oversees DSA review and approval and develops the review plan; DOE provides NRC technical staff the opportunity to observe DOE authorization reviews; DOE shares the documented safety analyses, technical data, and test results with NRC so they can be leveraged in subsequent NRC licensing; and NRC retains responsibility for licensing commercial advanced reactors. NRC Executive Director for Operations Mike King has disclosed that the commission has embedded twelve staff with DOE pilot projects so prior authorization work can be mapped to NRC requirements.
bullets: Fuel cycle: HALEU and used fuel handled under NRC 10 CFR Parts 70, 71, and 73
Export and security: 10 CFR Part 110 and Part 73 remain NRC administered
Section 202 of the Energy Reorganization Act: certain large demonstrations reserved to NRC
Primary purpose test: more than incidental grid sales push a pilot into NRC jurisdiction
NRC DOE MOU Addendum No. 9, signed October 24, 2025 (ML25303A288)
Twelve NRC staff embedded with DOE pilot projects to map authorization records to NRC requirements

sections.heading: Commercialization path post pilot
paragraphs: On April 2, 2026, the NRC published a proposed rule, "NRC Reviews of Reactor Designs Previously Authorized by U.S. Department of Energy or Department of War," at 91 Fed. Reg. (April 2, 2026). Comments closed May 4, 2026. The rule amends 10 CFR Section 50.43 to let Class 103 commercial applicants under Parts 50 and 52 directly reference a prior DOE or Department of War authorization, and adds a parallel pathway in 10 CFR Section 53.440 for applications under the new Part 53 framework, which became effective April 29, 2026.

The rule is an efficiency device, not a waiver. Applicants must map every element of the DOE authorization basis to the corresponding NRC requirement, re justify any change to design, functionality, hazards, site characteristics, or safety assumptions, and address NRC only topics that DOE does not adjudicate the same way. Those topics include financial qualifications, foreign ownership control or domination under Section 103d, decommissioning funding assurance under 10 CFR Section 50.75, emergency planning under 10 CFR Section 50.47 and Appendix E, operator licensing under Part 55, security under Part 73, and environmental review under NEPA via 10 CFR Part 51. Quality assurance, ITAAC where applicable, and reporting requirements under 10 CFR Section 50.55a, Section 50.59, and Part 21 must be satisfied by the DOE era record or supplemented.

For a developer running a DOE authorized pilot today, the realistic commercialization sequence is: reach criticality and accumulate operating, fuel performance, and post irradiation examination data under DOE authorization; use NRC observer access under Addendum No. 9 to keep NRC staff current on the DSA, design changes, and test results; file a commercial application under 10 CFR Part 50, Part 52 (standard design approval, design certification, early site permit, or combined license), or Part 53, invoking Section 50.43 or Section 53.440 to reference the DOE record; supplement with site specific safety analysis, environmental review, and the NRC only topics; and for fleet rollout, pursue a standard design certification or manufacturing license so site specific applications can reference the certified design.
bullets: NRC proposed rule published April 2, 2026, at 91 Fed. Reg.; comments closed May 4, 2026
Amends 10 CFR Section 50.43 (Parts 50 and 52) and 10 CFR Section 53.440 (Part 53)
Part 53 effective date: April 29, 2026
NRC retains independent reasonable assurance finding of adequate protection
Required supplements: financial qualifications, decommissioning funding, emergency planning, operator licensing, security, NEPA review
Fleet rollout vehicle: standard design certification or manufacturing license referenced in COL or Part 53 site applications

sections.heading: Industry reaction
paragraphs: The cross industry consensus is that the DOE pilot complements, rather than competes with, NRC licensing. American Nuclear Society Executive Director and CEO Craig Piercy said in March 2026 that the DOE pilot and NRC licensing "are not competing approaches. They are complementary strategies," adding that "for the first time in decades, the U.S. is not just talking about new nuclear, we are actively building toward it." An earlier ANS Expert Advisory Group review of the executive orders concluded that DOE and DOD "can play a constructive role in spurring construction of advanced reactors," but cautioned on the need to coordinate with the NRC "to avoid duplicative regulatory requirements, conflicting standards across agencies."

Nuclear Energy Institute President and CEO Maria Korsnick treated the pilot as one of several streams. In her 2026 State of the Nuclear Industry remarks, she said NRC modernization is "our launch pad" for nuclear growth, citing the Natrium 18 month construction permit and the Kairos Hermes review as evidence that the commission is moving at "unprecedented speed" without compromising safety. "Efficiency is not the opposite of safety," she said. "In fact, efficiency often enhances safety by keeping the focus of NRC reviews on those important areas."

Participating developers framed the pilot as a feeder into eventual commercial licensing rather than a substitute. Oklo CEO Jacob DeWitte called the selection a structural shift in which DOE is "unleashing its unique capabilities to enable American nuclear innovators to build." Terrestrial Energy CEO Simon Irish said Project TETRA "sits on our development center path, allowing the company to expedite key elements of its program to prepare licensing applications for commercial plant operation." Aalo Atomics CEO Matt Loszak said DOE's concierge team reduces approval cycles "from weeks to days," leaving engineering execution as the rate limiting step.

Kairos Power, NuScale, and TerraPower, the three most established advanced reactor developers, did not seek pilot slots. Kairos broke ground on Hermes 2 in April 2026 under its NRC construction permit and continues its $303 million ARDP cost share. NuScale's May 29, 2025 NRC Standard Design Approval for the 77 MWe US460 was treated as validation of the NRC pathway. TerraPower's NRC Natrium construction permit, issued in March 2026, is the first NRC construction permit for a commercial non light water reactor. NRC Chair Ho Nieh captured the regulator's posture: "If another federal agency has done the heavy lifting, the NRC will not make reactor developers start over from scratch," while insisting that "the NRC through this process will make and own its decisions. No other federal agency is going to tell NRC what decision to make."

sections.heading: What compliance teams should do today
paragraphs: For developers in the pilot, sponsors evaluating advanced reactor investments, and EHS or regulatory counsel at sites that may host follow on commercial plants, the right posture today is to treat the DOE authorization record as the substrate for the eventual NRC application, not as a stand alone deliverable. The April 2, 2026 NRC proposed rule rewards developers who build a defensible bridge between the two regimes from day one.
bullets: Build a requirements traceability matrix mapping every DSA element to the corresponding NRC requirement under Part 50, Part 52, or Part 53 before authorization is issued, not after

Maintain a configuration management baseline that captures every design, hazard, or site assumption change relative to the DOE reviewed scope, with each change pre tagged for re justification under Section 50.43 or Section 53.440

Stand up NRC only workstreams now: financial qualifications, decommissioning funding under 10 CFR Section 50.75, emergency planning under 10 CFR Section 50.47 and Appendix E, operator licensing under Part 55, security plans under Part 73, and NEPA scoping under 10 CFR Part 51
Use the NRC observer access granted under MOU Addendum No. 9 (ML25303A288) deliberately: brief NRC technical staff alongside DOE reviewers, log every observation, and resolve issues in the DOE record
Treat 10 CFR Part 21 reporting, 10 CFR Section 50.59 change control discipline, and an NRC grade quality assurance program as governing even though the pilot operates under DOE Order 414.1D
Verify fuel cycle interfaces (Parts 70, 71, 73) and export controls (Part 110) are routed to NRC counterparts in parallel with the DOE authorization workflow

Track the primary purpose test: keep grid sales incidental, document the research and development purpose of any energy output, and escalate to NRC pre application engagement before any commercial offtake

Calendar the Part 53 effective date (April 29, 2026) and the comment closure on the April 2, 2026 proposed rule (May 4, 2026); plan for a final rule that may further refine Section 50.43 and Section 53.440 leveraging

faqs.question: What is the DOE Advanced Reactor Pilot Program?
faqs.answer: The DOE Advanced Reactor Pilot Program is an expedited Department of Energy authorization pathway, established by Executive Order 14301 on May 23, 2025, to bring at least three privately funded advanced test reactors to criticality outside the national laboratories by July 4, 2026. DOE issued the Request for Application on June 18, 2025 and announced 11 selected projects from 10 companies on August 12, 2025. Authorization rests on DOE's Atomic Energy Act authority under 10 CFR Part 830, not on Nuclear Regulatory Commission commercial licensing. Applicants fund the full lifecycle of their reactors; DOE provides the regulatory pathway and assistance teams.

faqs.question: Which 11 reactors were selected in the DOE pilot?
faqs.answer: DOE announced 11 projects on August 12, 2025: Aalo Atomics Aalo-X Critical Test Reactor, Antares Nuclear Mark-0, Atomic Alchemy (an Oklo subsidiary) VIPR, Deep Fission Borehole Reactor-1, Last Energy PWR-5, Natura Resources MSR-1, Oklo Aurora-INL, a second Oklo award associated with the Aurora Fuel Fabrication Facility lineage, Radiant Industries Kaleidos Demonstration Unit, Terrestrial Energy Project TETRA, and Valar Atomics Ward 250. Oklo received two awards, including one for Atomic Alchemy. None of the established large advanced reactor vendors such as Kairos, NuScale, TerraPower, X energy, BWXT, Holtec, or Westinghouse were selected in this round.

faqs.question: What is the July 4, 2026 criticality target?
faqs.answer: Section 5(a) of Executive Order 14301 directs the Secretary of Energy to approve at least three reactors under the pilot with the goal of achieving criticality in each of the three by July 4, 2026. The date is a programmatic goal, not a statutory deadline, but DOE treats it as the headline metric. It gives selected applicants roughly 13 months from the August 12, 2025 selection to first criticality, an aggressive horizon by historical norms in which advanced reactor demonstrations have typically required 5 to 10 years from authorization to first criticality.

faqs.question: Has any pilot reactor reached criticality yet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Antares Nuclear's Mark-0, a 500 kWt sodium heat pipe cooled microreactor, achieved first criticality on June 3, 2026 at Idaho National Laboratory's Reactor and Critical Experiment (RACE) facility, the first novel reactor design to do so at INL in more than 50 years and the first private advanced reactor to reach criticality under the pilot program. Valar Atomics' NOVA Core test assembly reached criticality at Los Alamos National Laboratory's NCERC on November 17, 2025, the first criticality under the program, although at a national laboratory criticality facility rather than the Ward 250 power unit itself. Both events were zero power criticalities.

faqs.question: Does the DOE pilot bypass NRC licensing?
faqs.answer: It bypasses NRC commercial licensing for the pilot reactor itself. Authorization rests on DOE's standalone Atomic Energy Act authority under Section 31 and Section 91, the Section 110 statutory exemption from NRC Section 101 licensing, and the implementing regulation at 10 CFR Part 830. It does not bypass NRC for the eventual commercial fleet, for special nuclear material and fuel cycle interfaces under 10 CFR Parts 70, 71, and 73, for export controls under Part 110, or for reactors whose primary purpose is commercial or utility scale power generation. NRC jurisdiction attaches whenever grid sales become more than incidental.

faqs.question: What is the NRC's role under the pilot?
faqs.answer: The NRC is a coordination partner, not the licensing authority for the pilot reactor itself. The 2019 NRC DOE Memorandum of Understanding on advanced reactors, expanded by Addendum No. 9 signed October 24, 2025 (ADAMS ML25303A288), gives NRC technical staff observer access to DOE authorization reviews and routes documented safety analyses, technical data, and test results to NRC for use in any later commercial licensing. NRC Executive Director for Operations Mike King has disclosed that the commission has embedded twelve staff with DOE pilot projects. NRC retains independent licensing authority for commercial advanced reactors.

faqs.question: What does the April 2, 2026 NRC proposed rule do?
faqs.answer: On April 2, 2026, the NRC published a proposed rule, "NRC Reviews of Reactor Designs Previously Authorized by U.S. Department of Energy or Department of War," at 91 Fed. Reg. (April 2, 2026). Comments closed May 4, 2026. The rule amends 10 CFR Section 50.43 to let Class 103 commercial applicants under Parts 50 and 52 reference a prior DOE or Department of War authorization, and adds a parallel pathway at 10 CFR Section 53.440 for the new Part 53 framework, which became effective April 29, 2026. The rule is an evidentiary shortcut, not a waiver. NRC still makes the reasonable assurance finding of adequate protection.

faqs.question: What is the statutory basis for DOE reactor authorization?
faqs.answer: DOE authorizes reactors under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, as amended. Section 31 (42 U.S.C. Section 2051) authorizes DOE to conduct research and development on atomic energy, including experimental reactors. Section 91 (42 U.S.C. Section 2121) supports atomic energy programs. Section 110 (42 U.S.C. Section 2140) exempts DOE from the Section 101 NRC licensing requirement for activities under DOE contracts. The Energy Reorganization Act of 1974 and the DOE Organization Act of 1977 reinforce the Secretary of Energy's self regulatory authority. Implementing regulations sit at 10 CFR Part 830, with safety basis methodology in DOE-STD-3009 and DOE-STD-1189.

faqs.question: Why did Kairos, NuScale, and TerraPower stay out of the pilot?
faqs.answer: The three most established advanced reactor developers were already advanced on the NRC track and treated commercial licensing as the credibility anchor for offtakers and lenders. Kairos broke ground on Hermes 2 in April 2026 under its NRC construction permit and continues its $303 million ARDP cost share. NuScale's May 29, 2025 NRC Standard Design Approval for the 77 MWe US460 was treated as bankability validation. TerraPower's Natrium received the first NRC construction permit for a commercial non light water reactor in March 2026. Their absence from the pilot is itself a signal that the NRC pathway remains the route to commercial deployment.

faqs.question: How does the pilot fit with the broader May 23, 2025 nuclear executive order package?
faqs.answer: EO 14301 is one of four orders signed on May 23, 2025. The others are EO 14302, "Reinvigorating the Nuclear Industrial Base" (fuel cycle, HALEU, plutonium repurposing, workforce, Defense Production Act authority), "Deploying Advanced Nuclear Reactor Technologies" (Department of Defense and federal site deployments), and the order "Ordering the Reform of the Nuclear Regulatory Commission" (18 month license decision cap and NRC restructuring). The package targets a quadrupling of installed U.S. nuclear capacity from roughly 100 GW to 400 GW by 2050, with 5 GW of power uprates and 10 new large reactors under construction by 2030 as interim milestones.

faqs.question: Who funds the pilot reactors?
faqs.answer: The applicants do. Per the June 18, 2025 Request for Application, selected developers bear the full lifecycle cost of design, manufacturing, construction, operations, and decommissioning. DOE provides the regulatory pathway, assistance teams, and authorization, not capital. Some developers have closed substantial private rounds tied to pilot execution: Last Energy is backed by a $100 million Series C; Radiant Industries closed a $300 million plus raise in December 2025. Several pilot projects also intersect with DOE's parallel Fuel Line Pilot Program and Defense Production Act Consortium for fuel supply chain support, established under EO 14302.

faqs.question: What should compliance teams do now to prepare for commercial licensing?
faqs.answer: Build a requirements traceability matrix mapping every Documented Safety Analysis element to the corresponding NRC requirement under Part 50, Part 52, or Part 53 before DOE authorization issues. Maintain a configuration management baseline tagged for re justification under 10 CFR Section 50.43 or Section 53.440. Stand up NRC only workstreams now: financial qualifications, decommissioning funding under 10 CFR Section 50.75, emergency planning under 10 CFR Section 50.47 and Appendix E, operator licensing under Part 55, security under Part 73, and NEPA scoping under 10 CFR Part 51. Treat 10 CFR Part 21, Section 50.59, and NRC grade quality assurance as governing from day one.
